Facts.text: The 4th Caliph of the Rashidun Caliphate, Ali ibn Abu Talib is struck on the head while praying at the Great Mosque of Kufa, Mesopotamia by a poison-coated sword wielded by Ibn Muljam a Kharijite, dies two days later
St Vitalian ends his reign as Catholic Pope
Sergius II ends his reign as Catholic Pope
Wrongful execution of noted Song Dynasty General Yue Fei
Dante becomes a Florentine political exile
Pope Clement VI issues the Bull Unigenitus
French KingFrancis Isigns classified treaty with Landgrave of Hesse
Dutchy of Guelders accepts rule of William, Duke of Jülich-Cleves-Berg
Vatican opens blasphemy and theological heresy trial against Italian scholar and wayward friar Giordano Bruno; proceedings drag on for over 7 years

English High Court of Justice finds King Charles I "guilty of the crimes of which he had been accused, did judge him tyrant, traitor, murderer, and public enemy to the good people of the nation, to be put to death by the severing of his head from his body"
1st American lime kiln begins operation in Providence, Rhode Island
Niccolò Piccinni's tragic opera "Roland" premieres at Théâtre du Palais-Royale in Paris, France
1st US state university chartered in Athens, Georgia
President Monroe appoints first US ambassadors to South America
US whaling and sealing vessel the Levant captained by Mercator Cooper, makes the first known landing on mainland Antarctica at Oates Coast, Victoria land
Battle of Fair Gardens, Tennessee
Civil War skirmish at Kelly's Ford, Virginia
1st sorority (Kappa Alpha Theta) (DePauw U in Greencastle, Indiana)
After accepting 15th amendment, Virginia is readmitted to the Union
Manitoba & Northwest Territories incorporated
First British government of Salisbury resigns
Mine explosion kills 109 at Mount Pleasant Pennsylvania
1st college basketball game, University of Chicago beats Chicago YMCA 19-11
Midwinter Fair opens in Golden Gate Park
Tasmania bowl out Victoria for 65 for their 1st ever innings victory
British troops occupy Bida Gold Coast (Ghana)
Foreign diplomats in Peking, China, write formal notes of protest demanding that the Chinese Government stop the Boxers and other groups leading attacks on Westerners and Christians.
Social Democrat Party of America (Debs' party) holds 1st convention
5 workers killed on explosion during IRT subway construction (NYC)
Maurice Rouvier forms government in France
Rudolf Gundersen skates world record 500m at 44.8 sec
Pasiphaë, a satellite of Jupiter, discovered by Melotte
The Young Left is founded in Norway
A petition is written and submitted by the black and coloured women of the Orange Free State, an independent Boer sovereign republic in southern Africa, against the carrying of passes by women
US Marines occupy Haiti
Communist party "Spartacus Letters" 1st published in Berlin
Coen de Koning wins 2nd official 11 cities race (9:53) (record)
The first hostilities occurred in the Finnish Civil War.
Egyptian king Foead nominates Saad Zaghloel Pasja premier
The Kingdom of Italy and the Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes sign the Treaty of Rome, agreeing to the annexation of independent Free State of Fiume by Italy (now Rijeka, Croatia)
The Natal Indian Congress and the Natal Indian Association jointly organise a mass meeting in Durban, South Africa in opposition to the Class Areas Bill
US Senate agrees to join World Court
Otto Meisnner (Head of the German President's Office) dines with British ambassador Sir Horace Rumbold
French government of Chautemps falls (Stavisky Affair)
VARA refuses to hire after commemoration of Marinus Van de Lubbe
First flight of the Lockheed P-38 Lightning
-17°F (-27°C), CCC Camp F-16, Georgia (state record)
-19°F (-27.4°C) is the coldest day in the Netherlands since 1850
1st US air attack on Germany (Wilhelmshafen)
Nazi occupiers forbid food transport to West (The Netherlands)
S Romberg, H&D Fields' musical premieres in NYC
Wally van Hall, Dutch banker and resistance leader, arrested
1st locomotive to carry 1,000,000 pounds (450,000 kg) operates
1st tape recorder sold
Chinese liner "Taiping" collides with a collier off south China
2nd Emmy Awards: Ed Wynn Show & Texaco Star Theater win
US begins 126 nuclear tests at Nevada Test Site
Netherlands end Marshall aid
1955 NFL Draft: George Shaw from University of Oregon first pick by Baltimore Colts
NFL's New York Giants move their home from the Polo Grounds in Upper Manhattan to Yankee Stadium in The Bronx
Veikko Hakulinen of Finland wins Olympic 30k cross country gold medal at Cortina d'Ampezzo; second career Olympic title after winning the 15k event in Oslo, 1952
Ferenc Munnich succeeds Kadar as premier of Hungary
"Sing Along with Mitch" [Miller] premieres on NBC TV
New Zealander Peter Snell sets a new world mile record, running in 3 minutes 54.4 seconds at Wanganui's Cook Gardens
Sam Rice, Eppa Rixey, Elmer Flick, & John Clarkson elected to Baseball Hall of Fame
Yevgeni Grishin skates world record 500m in 39.6 sec
Australian cricketers Eddie Barlow and Graeme Pollock combine for a 341 stand at Adelaide Oval
US Senator Margaret Chase Smith of Maine announces her bid for Republican presidential nomination
1st ground station-to-aircraft radio communication via satellite
Groundbreaking for "Dragon Gateway" at Grant Avenue
Wisconsin State Circuit Court Judge Elmer W Roller rules either the Braves stay in Milwaukee or NL must promise Wisconsin an expansion team for 1966
New Orleans Saints sign their 1st player, kicker Paige Cothren
The Beatles sign a 9-year worldwide contract with EMI records
Treaty signed banning military use of nuclear weapons in space
French submarine Minerve disappears in the Mediterranean with the loss of 52 crew
14 spies hanged in Baghdad
9 Jews publicly executed in Damascus, Syria
Actress Thelma Ritter suffers a heart attack (she dies Feb 5th)
Movie rating system modifies "M" rating to "PG"
NFL Draft: Louisiana Tech quarterback Terry Bradshaw #1 pick by Pittsburgh Steelers
Montgomery St Station, last link in Bay Area Rapid Transit in San Francisco, 'holed thru'
The body of a man who had been shot dead is found in Belfast
The British Army and the Irish Republican Army engage in gun battles near County Armagh; British troops fire over 1,000 rounds of ammunition
Two Royal Ulster Constabulary officers shot dead by IRA in an attack on their patrol car in the Creggan Road, Derry

Paris Peace Accords signed by US Secretary of State William P. Rogers, (North) Vietnam Minister for Foreign Affairs Nguyen Duy Trinh, Republic of South Vietnam Minister of Foreign Affairs Nguyễn Thị Bình, and Republic of Vietnam Minister for Foreign Affairs Trần Văn Lắm, ending America's then longest war and it's military draft.
UCLA's basketball team wins 61st consecutive game (NCAA record)
Morocco-Algeria battles in West Sahara
Islanders ends 23 undefeated games at home streak (15-0-8)
Los Angeles Kings set an NHL record with their 6th consecutive tied game, a 4-4 deadlock against the visiting Philadelphia Flyers
Mauno Koivisto installed as President of Finland
Roberto S. Cordova installed as President of Honduras
Pilot tunnel breaks through under the Tsugaru Strait, for Seikan Tunnel project to connect Honshu and Hokkaido, Japan, by rail
15th Space Shuttle (51-C) Mission-Discovery 3 returns to Earth
Mark Mckoy cycles world record 50m hurdles indoor (5.25)
Midnight Rockers beat Buddy Rose & Doug Somers for AWA World Tag Team
German war criminals Fischer & Aus der Funten freed
Kevin Johnson (Phoenix) begins NBA free throw streak of 57 games
Oklahoma linebacker Mark VanKeirsblilck assaults an Oklahoma grad student
Dutch Pacifist Socialistic Party disbands
Nadine Strossen is 1st female president of the ACLU
Super Bowl XXV, Tampa Stadium, Tampa, FL: New York Giants beat Buffalo Bills, 20-19; MVP: Ottis Anderson, NY Giants, RB
DC-3 crashes in Kinshasa, killing 12
Carlos Reina succeeds pres Callejas in Honduras
Romanian social democrats form government with anti-Semites
Eric Cantona of Manchester United fined and banned from playing football after attacking a fan
15 day old conjoined twins separated: Sarah Morales survives, Sarahi dies
Catherine Roskam becomes the 1st NY female Episcopal bishop
Colonel Ibrahim Baré Maïnassara deposes the first democratically elected President of Niger, Mahamane Ousmane, in a military coup
France performs nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
Germany celebrates its 1st Holocaust Remembrance Day
Shiv Chanderpaul scores 303* for Guyana v Jamaica at Kingston
"Men Are From Mars, Women Are From Venus" opens at Gershwin NYC
Crane crashes into Roosevelt Island tram (NYC), injuring 10
Roland Clarkson discovers 2^3021377-1 (37th known Mersenne prime)
WNBA begins filling rosters of Washington Mystics & Detroit Shock
7 people are killed and 3 are injured when a retaining wall collapses during a football match at the Ugur Sports Facility, Turkey
A bus crash into a ravine in Serta, Portugal, kills 11 people and injures an additional 32
A series of bomb attacks in Kandahar, Afghanistan, kills 20 police officers
In Port Said, Egypt, protests result in 7 people being killed and 630 are injured
Spain defeats Denmark to win the 2013 World Men's Handball Championship

The Kiss nightclub fire in Santa Maria, Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil, ignited by pyrotechnics used by a performing rock band kills 245 people and injures at least 600 others; most of the deaths were from toxic smoke inhalation, the club was over capacity and had no fire exits

On This Day receives its second substantial face-lift, courtesy of a fresh new website design and brand by design studio Fhoke. A dedicated film and television channel is launched alongside the new design.
Bomb in an ambulance kills over 100 people in Kabul, Taliban claim responsibility
Landslide kills 15 at a wedding party in a hotel in Abancay, Peru
Tornado strikes Havana, Cuba, killing three and injuring 172
Two bombs at a Roman Catholic cathedral on Jolo Island, southern, Philippines kills 20, Islamic State claims responsibility
Former king of Belgium, Albert II admits fathering a child after DNA tests confirm paternity

International Holocaust Remembrance Day: representatives from various countries gather at site of the Auschwitz-Birkenau concentration camp; among attendees is Doug Emhoff, the first Jewish spouse of a US president or vice president
New Zealand city of Auckland declares a state of emergency as a tropical storm brings record levels of rain, major flooding and four deaths
Urgent search announced for tiny radioactive capsule 6mmx8mm, which dropped out of a truck somewhere along 1,400 km journey in Western Australia
World's largest cruise ship Icon of the Seas, able to house 7,600 passengers, at 365 meters (1,198 ft) 5x larger than the Titanic, sets sail from Miami on its maiden voyage
James McHenry, the acting US Attorney General, fires more than a dozen prosecutors who worked for on special counsel Jack Smith's prosecution ofDonald Trump
Rwanda-backed rebels, led by the Tutsi group M23, seize Goma, the biggest city in eastern Democratic Republic of Congo, displacing 500,000 people
Stocks of Nvidia, leading supplier of AI chips, lose $588.8 billion in market value — the most a stock has ever lost in a single day, after the release of a Chinese AI model by DeepSeek
Trajanbecomes Roman Emperor after the death ofNerva.
William I of Orangebecomes a Knight of the Order of the Golden Fleece
Scottish schoolmaster Dr. John Fian burned for witchcraft at Castle Hill, Edinburgh by order KingJames VI. Part of the Berwick witch trials.
Welsh pirateHenry Morganlands at the gates of Panama City
Charles Perrault's poem "The Age of Louis the Great" read out at the French Academy, part of the literary quarrel of the Ancients and the Moderns
Mustafa IIbecomes the Ottoman sultan in Istanbul on the death of Ahmed II. Mustafa rules until his death in 1703.
TsarPeter the Greatsets first Russian state budget
Thomas Edisonpatents electric incandescent lamp
Marius Petipa and Lev Ivanov's revival of PyotrTchaikovsky's ballet "Swan Lake" premieres in St. Petersburg
"Tarzanof the Apes", 1st Tarzan film, premieres at Broadway Theater
Vladimir Leninis placed in a Mausoleum in Red Square, Moscow
PhysicistErwin Schrödingerpublishes his theory of wave mechanics and presents what becomes known as the Schrödinger equation in quantum mechanics
Casey Stengel, manager of the Boston Braves since 1938, resigns Lou Perini, Guido Rugo, & Joseph Maney buy control of Boston Braves
Soviet troops liberateAuschwitzand Birkenau Concentration Camps in Poland -  now commemorated as International Holocaust Remembrance Day
American boxerArchie Moorebeats Joey Maxim in 15-round unanimous decision to retain his world light heavyweight title at the Orange Bowl, Miami; last of famous trilogy of fights, all won by Moore
RCA records releasesElvis Presley's single "Heartbreak Hotel", his first million-seller (written by Mae Boren Axton and Tommy Durden)
Leontyne Priceand Franco Corelli make debuts at the Metropolitan Opera in New York in "Il trovatore"; final curtain call lasts 35 minutes
Chuck Nollis named head coach of the Pittsburgh Steelers (the youngest coach in NFL history at the time)
ReverendIan Paisleysentenced to 3 to three months in jail at Armagh for participating in unlawful assembly during civil rights march (Nov. 30)
World premiere ofScott Joplin's rediscovered opera "Treemonisha" at Morehouse College in Atlanta
"Laverne & Shirley" TV spinoff from "Happy Days" starringPenny Marshalland Cindy Williams premieres on ABC TV
Robert Mugabereturns to Rhodesia after 5 years in exile
CBS airs "The Bunker", based on journalist James P. O'Donnell's book of the same name, starringAnthony Hopkinsas Adolf Hitler, in an Emmy Award-winning performance
Philadelphia Phillies trade shortstop Larry Bowa & infielderRyne Sandbergto Chicago Cubs for 2nd baseman Ivan DeJesus
Geffen Records releases "Milk & Honey" album byJohn LennonandYoko Ono, featuring un-released recordings made during the 1980 "Double Fantasy" sessions, including "Grow Old With Me"
Michael Jacksonis burned during filming for Pepsi commercial
Senate Judiciary Committee unanimously approves nomination of JudgeAnthony Kennedyto US Supreme Court
Detroit Red Wings centerSteve Yzermanbecomes the 4th NHL player to record 100 points in 50 games or less with a goal and 2 assists as Red Wings beat Toronto, 8-1
Jane Fondaundergoes arthroscopic surgery on her right knee
Mike Tysongoes on trial for rape (he is found guilty)
Presidential candidateBill Clinton(D) & Genifer Flowers accuse each other of lying over her assertion they had a 12-year affair
"Yeah!" single released byUsherfeaturing Lil Jon and Ludacris (Grammy Award Best Rap/Sung Collaboration 2005, Billboard Song of the Year 2004)
Columbia Records releasesBruce Springsteen's 16th studio album "Working On A Dream", made with the E Street Band
Magnus Carlsenwins the 2013 Tata Steel Chess Tournament in Wijk aan Zee, Netherlands
Donald Trumpissues executive order banning travel to the US for 7 mostly Muslim countries and suspending admission for refugees
US PresidentJoe Bidenpledges to nominate a Black woman to the Supreme Court
Russian Antarctic expedition led byFabian Gottlieb von Bellingshausenand Mikhail Lazarev discover the continent of Antarctica
US PresidentJames Monroeurges Congress to approve creation of Indian Territory, west of the Mississippi River, for the relocation of Eastern Indian tribes to 'promote their welfare and happiness"
Clas Thunbergof Finland claims 3 gold medals in one day when he wins the 1,500m, 5,000m and all-round speed skating events at the Chamonix Winter Olympics; won 5,000m the day before
Peruvian ambassador Ricardo Rivera-Schreiber warns American Ambassador of Japanese assault on Pearl Harbor
Siege of Leningrad lifted by the Soviets after 880 days and more than 2 million Russians killed
Fire in NASA's Apollo 1 Command Module kills astronauts Gus Grissom, Ed White, and Roger B. Chaffee during a launch rehearsal
Albert III, Duke of Saxony, born in Grimma, Saxony (d. 1500)
Joachim Frederick, German Prince-Elector of Brandenburg (1598-1608), born in Cölln (d. 1608)
Abbas I, Abbas the Great, Shah of Persia who made Eṣfahān his capital (1588-1629), born in Herat, Safavid Iran (d. 1629)
Hendrick Avercamp, Dutch painter, specialized in winter landscapes, born in Amsterdam (d. 1634)
Harbottle Grimston, English politician, Speaker of the House of Parliament during the Civil War, born at Bradfield Hall, near Manningtree (d. 1685)
Thomas Willis, English physician, studied epidemiology, anatomy of the brain and the nervous system, born in  Great Bedwyn, England (d. 1675)
Richard Bentley, English classical scholar (Dissertation on the Epistles of Phalaris), started the 1st written examinations in the West, born in Oulton, England (d. 1742)
Jean-François de Troy, French painter (Rococo style), born in Paris, France (d. 1752)
Johann Nicholas von Hontheim, German church historian and theologian (febronianism), born in Trier (d. 1790)
Vaclav Kalous [Simon à Scto Bartholomaeo], Czech composer, born in Solnice (d. 1786)
Samuel Foote, British actor, dramatist and theatre manager (Mirror), born in Truro, Cornwall (d. 1777)
Johann Andreas Cramer, German preacher (Court Preacher to King Frederick V of Denmark), born in Jönstadt (d. 1788)
Hester Thrale, Welsh diarist, friend of Samuel Johnson and source for his life, born at Caernarvonshire, Wales (d. 1821)
Martin-Joseph Mengal, Belgian composer, conductor, and educator (Director of the Royal Conservatory of Ghent, 1835-51), born in Ghent, Belgium (d. 1851)
Juan Álvarez, Mexican revolutionary leader (President of Mexico 1855), born in Concepción de Atoyac, Mexico (d. 1867)
Maria Anna of Bavaria, queen consort of Saxony, born in Munich, Bavaria (d. 1877)
Samuel Palmer, British painter and etcher (Valley of Vision), born in London, England (d. 1881)
Sophie of Bavaria, German-born Archduchess of Austria, born in Nymphenburg Palace, Munich (d. 1872)
Juan Crisóstomo Arriaga, Basque child prodigy and composer, born in Bilbao, Spain (d. 1826)
David Strauss, German theologist (focused on historical Jesus' Life), born in Ludwigsburg (d. 1874)
Eugène Viollet-le-Duc, French architect (restored medieval monuments including city of Carcassonne), born in Paris, France (d. 1879)
Thomas Leiper Kane, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1883)
Édouard Lalo, French composer (Symphonie Espagnole; Le roi d'Ys), born in Lille, France (d. 1892)
Jozef Israels, Dutch landscape painter, born in Groningen, Netherlands (d. 1911)
Richard Taylor, American Confederate general and Louisiana Senator, brother-in-law of Jefferson Davis, born at Springfield, Kentucky (d. 1879)
Louis Schubert, German, violinist, composer, and pedagogue, born in Dessau, Duchy of Anhalt, German Confederation (d. 1884)
Samuel Allen Rice, American Attorney General of Iowa and Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Cattaraugus, New York (d. 1864)
Georg Hellmesberger Jr, Austrian violinist and composer, born in Vienna, Austrian Empire (d. 1852)
William Henry Fitzhugh Payne, American lawyer and soldier (Brigadier General in Confederate Army), born in Fauquier County, Virginia (d. 1904)
Robert Sanford Foster, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Vernon, Indiana (d. 1903)
Leopold von Sacher-Masoch, Austrian writer (word masochism derived from his name), born in Lemberg, the capital of the Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria, Austrian Empire (d. 1895)
Arkhip Kuindzhi, Ukrainian landscape painter, born in Mariupol, Russian Empire (d. 1910)
Tōgō Heihachirō, Imperial Japanese Navy admiral and one of Japan's greatest naval heroes, born in Kajiya-Chō, Kagoshima-Jōka, Satsuma, Japan (d. 1934)
John Collier, English painter (Lady Godiva) and writer, born in Paddington, England (d. 1934)
Rafael Obligado, Argentine, writer (Santos Vega), born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 1920)
Neel Doff, Dutch born Belgian writer (Days of Hunger and Distress)and artist's model, born in Buggenum, Netherlands (d. 1842)
Claude Terrasse, French organist and operetta composer, born in L’Arbresle, Rhône, France (d. 1923)
Cato Engelen-Sewing, Dutch soprano prima donna (Dutch Opera), born in Amsterdam (d. 1961)
Will Marion Cook, American composer, violinist and choral director (In Dahomey), born in Washington D.C. (d. 1944)
Learned Hand, American Chief Judge of US Court of Appeals, born in Albany, New York (d. 1961)
François Rasse, Belgian violinist, composer, and pedagogue, born in Helchin, Belgium (d. 1955)
Sven Holger Körling, Swedish organist, music teacher, and composer of children's songs, born in Ystad, Sweden (d. 1948)
Giuseppe Prezzolini, Italian-American writer (La Voce, La Cultura Italiano), born in Perugia, Italy (d. 1982)
Eduard Künneke, German conductor and theater composer, born in Emmerich, Germany (d. 1953)
Maeda Seison, Japanese traditional Yamato-e painter, born in Nakatsugawa city, Japan (d. 1977)
Frank Nitti [Francesco Nitto], Italian-American gangster, born in Angri, Campania, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1943)
Carl Blegen, American archaeologist (excavator at Troy, Pylos), born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 1971)
Balthasar van der Pol, Dutch physicist (Comparison of Van der Pol), born in Utrecht, Netherlands (d. 1959)
Mitya Stillman, Russian-American violist and composer, born in Ukraine, Russian Empire (d. 1936)
Soong Ching-ling, Chinese political leader of the  Kuomintang and wife of Sun Yat-sen, born in Shanghai (d. 1981)
Albert Patterson, American politician and attorney, born in New Site, Alabama (d. 1954)
Fritz Pollard, early African American NFL star (1920-26) and coach, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1986)
Cláudio Carneyro, Portuguese violinist, composer, and educator, born in Porto, Portugal (d. 1963)
Harry Ruby [Harry Rubenstein], American composer and screenwriter, born in New York City (d. 1974)
Joseph Rosenstock, American conductor (NY City Opera), born in Kraków, Poland (d. 1985)

Béla Guttmann, Hungarian soccer midfielder (4 caps; MTK Hungária, Hakoah Wien) and coach (Benfica, AC Milan, FC Porto; Hungary, Austria, Netherlands, Italy, Brazil, Uruguay, Portugal), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1981)
Granville English, American composer, born in Louisville, Kentucky (d. 1968)
Sulo Nikolai Salonen, Finnish composer, born in Pyhtää (d. 1976)
Hyman G. Rickover, US Admiral (father of modern nuclear navy, at 63 years the longest serving US naval officer), born in Maków Mazowiecki, Congress Poland (d. 1986)
Art Rooney, American NFL Pittsburgh Steelers [Pirates] team founder, owner, and chairman (1933-88), born in Coulterville, Pennsylvania (d. 1988)
Willy Fritsch, German actor (Spies, Women in the Moon), born in Katowice, Upper Silesia, Germany (d. 1973)
John Eccles, Australian neurologist (1963 Nobel Prize for Physiology or Medicine for work on the synapse), born in Melbourne, Australia (d. 1997)
Otto P. Weyland, American air force commander during the Korean War, born in Riverside, California (d. 1979)
Howard McNear, American actor (The Andy Griffith Show, Anatomy of a Murder, Irma La Douce), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1969)
Luther Diamond, American radio personality, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1994)
Alberto Ruz Lhuillier, Mexican archaeologist who excavated Palenque, born in Paris, France (d. 1979)
Radames Gnattali, Brazilian composer and conductor (Rádio Nacional), born in Porto Alegre, Brazil (d. 1988)
Oran "Hot Lips" Page, American jazz trumpeter, singer, and bandleader, born in Dallas, Texas (d. 1954)
William Randolph Hearst, Jr., American newspaper magnate (Hearst Newspapers) and 1955 Pulitzer Prize winner, born in Manhattan, New York City (d. 1993)
Benay Venuta [Benvenuta Rose Crooke], American actress, singer and dancer (Annie Get Your Gun, Call Me Mister), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1995)
Arne Næss, Norwegian philosopher, born in Oslo, Norway (d. 2009)
E. R. Braithwaite, Guyanese diplomat and writer (To Sir with Love), born in Georgetown, British Guiana (d. 2016)
Milton Adolphus, American pianist and composer, born in the Bronx, New York (d. 1988)
Valery Zhelobinsky, Russian pianist and composer, born in Tambov, Russia (d. 1946)
Anna Larina, Russian revolutionary and wife of Bolshevik leader Nikolai Bukharin, born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1996)
Jack Brymer, British classical clarinetist (Royal Philharmonic; BBC Symphony; London Symphony), and teacher, born in South Shields, England (d. 2003)
(Lyle) "Skitch" Henderson, British-American pianist and orchestra leader (NBC Radio; NBC Television; New York Pops), born in Birmingham, England (d. 2005)
Elmore James, American blues slide guitarist (Dust My Broom), born in Richland, Mississippi (d. 1963)
William Seawell, American Army Brigadier General and CEO of Pan Am, born in Pine Bluff, Arkansas (d. 2005)
David Seville [Ross Bagdasarian], American songwriter ("Come On A-My House"), singer (Alvin & The Chipmunks - "Witch Doctor"), and actor (Rear Window), born in Fresno, California (d. 1972)
Nina Milkina, Russian British pianist, born in Moscow (d. 2006)
Helmut Zacharias, German violinist, born in Berlin, Germany (d. 2002)
John Box, British Academy Award and BAFTA winning film production designer and art director (A Man for all Seasons; Oliver!), born in Highgate, London (d. 2005)
Donna Reed, American actress (From Here to Eternity; It's a Wonderful Life; The Donna Reed Show), born in Denison, Iowa (d. 1986)
Alexander Chugayev, Soviet Russian composer, born in Yeysk, USSR (d. 1990)
Brian Rix [Lord Rix], British actor and activist (Mencap), born in Cottingham, Yorkshire (d. 2016)
Rauf Denktaş, Turkish-Cypriot politician, founding President of North Cyprus (1983-2005), born in Paphos, British Cyprus (d. 2012)
Sabu [Dastagir], Indian actor (The Thief of Bagdad, Elephant Boy, Drum), born in Mysore, British India (d. 1963)
Geoffrey Tucker, British political consultant who helped Macmillan, Heath and Thatcher into office, born in Bristol, England (d. 2003)
John Bury, British stage designer known for his work with Peter Hall (The Wars of the Roses), born in Aberystwyth, Wales (d. 2000)
Ingrid Thulin, Swedish actress (Cries & Whispers, Damned), born in Sollefteå, Sweden (d. 2004)
Anna Ornstein, Hungarian-American Holocaust survivor and psychoanalyst (Survival and Recovery), born in Szendro, Hungary (d. 2025)[1]
Doretta Morrow, American actress (Because You're Mine), born in New York City (d. 1968)
Michael Craig [Gregson], British actor (Escape 2000; Vault of Horror), and screenwriter (The Fourth Wish), born in Poona, British India
Nancy Dickerson, pioneering American radio and TV journalist (NBC, CBS), born in Wauwatosa, Wisconsin (d. 1997)
Hans Modrow, German politician (last Communist Prime Minister of East Germany 1989-90), born in Jasenitz, Germany (d. 2023)
Jean-Michel Damase, French pianist and composer, born in Bordueax (d. 2013)
Vaclovas Paketūras, Lithuanian composer (The Sun Is Setting; Polyphonic Pieces), born in Gruodiškiai, Lithuania (d. 2018)
Bill English, American computer engineer who helped develop the computer mouse and the revolutionary NLS computer system, born in Lexington, Kentucky (d. 2020)
Gastón Suárez, Bolivian novelist and dramatist, born in Tupiza, Bolivia (d. 1984)
Mohamed Al Fayed, Egyptian business magnate (Harrods - London; The Ritz Hotel - Paris), born in Alexandria, Egypt (d. 2023)
Richard Du Cann, English lawyer and QC, born in London (d. 1993)
Bobby "Blue" Bland, American blues an soul singer ("Call on the Drummer"), born in Rosemark, Tennessee (d. 2013)
Esteban Edward Torres, American politician (Rep-D-CA, 1983-99), born in Miami, Arizona (d. 2022)
John Hopkins, English TV, film and stage writer (Z-Cars), born in London (d. 1998)
Mordecai Richler, Canadian author (The Apprenticeship of Duddy Kravitz), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2001)
Nigel Vinson, British Lord and inventor, born in Kent, England
Ronald "Buster" Edwards, British Great Train Robber turned flower-seller, born in London (d. 1994)
Rudi Maugeri, Canadian doo-wop vocalist (Crew Cuts - "Sh-Boom"), and broadcaster, born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 2004)
George Montgomery, American drag racer (7 x NHRA national title gas class; Motorsports HOF of America), born in Dayton, Ohio (d. 2023)
Jerry Buss [Gerald Hatten Buss], American businessman, philanthropist, and majority owner of the LA Lakers, born in Salt Lake City, Utah (d. 2013)
Edithe Cresson, French politician, 1st female Prime Minister of France (1991-92), born in Paris, France
Federico Mayor Zaragoza, Spanish scientist, scholar, politician, diplomat (UNESCO director, 1987-99), and poet, born in Barcelona, Spain (d. 2024)
Julian Ogilvie Thompson, South African businessman (CEO of De Beers Diamonds. 1985-97), born in Cape Town, Union of South Africa (d. 2023)
Raymond Boudon, French sociologist, born in Paris, France (d. 2013)
Gillian Beer, English literary critic (President of Clare Hall-Cambridge University), born in Surrey, England
Barry C. Barish, American Physicist (Nobel Prize 2017, gravitational waves), born in Omaha, Nebraska
Ismail Kadare, Albanian author and poet (Dimri i Madh), born in Gjirokastër, Kingdom of Albania (d. 2024)[1]
Troy Donahue, American actor (Surfside Six, Cockfighter, Hawaiian Eye), born in New York City (d. 2001)
John Ogdon, British pianist and composer, born in Mansfield Woodhouse, Nottinghamshire, England (d. 1989)
Mike Hill, American golfer (3 PGA; PGA 18 Senior titles), born in Jackson, Michigan (d. 2025)
Tigran Mansuryan, Armenian composer (The Tango of Our Childhood; Requiem), born in Beirut, Lebanon
Brian T O'Leary, astronaut, born in Boston, Massachusetts
James Cromwell, American actor (Babe, The Green Mile), born in Los Angeles, California
Bobby Hutcherson, American jazz vibraphone and marimba player ("Little B's Poem"; SFJAZZ Collective), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2016)
